results.possibleIdentity: Richard R. Black (1932–2020) was a prominent American artist, printmaker, and educator who was active from the 1960s through the late 20th century. He served as a Professor of Art and Head of Printmaking at Drake University for 34 years (1960–1994). In 1975, he founded the Drake University Biennial Print Symposium, which became a significant national forum for the art of printmaking. His work is characterized by the use of the intaglio process to create abstract, collage-like prints with intricate textures and enigmatic forms. Black's artistic practice involved experimental techniques, and his work is held in numerous museum collections, including the Des Moines Art Center and the Indianapolis Art Museum. He was a member of the Royal Society of Painter-Printmakers (RE) and received multiple honors, such as the Drake Award for excellence in teaching and artistic achievement.

Richard Black is an audio technology consultant, recording engineer, and pianist who became professionally active in the 1970s. With a background in physics, he worked for British Telecom and used highly sensitive equipment to research electrical theory, specifically impedance matching in speaker cables. He is recognized for his technical white papers on cable theory and audio reproduction, such as 'Cable Theory for Sceptics,' which he produced in collaboration with companies like Townshend Audio. His career bridges the fields of engineering and music, as he has worked as a producer, editor, and consultant on audio technology while maintaining a practice as a pianist. This multidisciplinary profile (matching the 'artist/engineer' collaboration model) aligns with the objectives of the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which promoted such partnerships during the late 1960s and 1970s.

Harry Richard Black (1921–2014) was a prolific American commercial artist and illustrator active during the mid-to-late 20th century. He is best known for creating the original 'Mr. Clean' mascot for Procter & Gamble in the 1950s and contributing to the visual identity of 'Smokey Bear' for the U.S. Forest Service. Throughout the late 1960s and 1970s, he maintained a commercial art studio in Dayton, Ohio, and taught art part-time at the University of Dayton (1967–1982) and Sinclair Community College. While primarily recognized for his iconic advertising characters, he was also a landscape and portrait painter whose work appeared in national publications like The Saturday Evening Post.

results.possibleIdentity: Kenneth Snelson (1927–2016) was an American sculptor and photographer best known for his pioneering work in "tensegrity" (a term coined by Buckminster Fuller, though Snelson developed the concept), which he also referred to as "floating compression." His sculptures typically consist of rigid aluminum or stainless steel tubes held in place by a network of flexible cables, creating a dynamic equilibrium where no two rigid members touch. Snelson was active and achieved significant critical acclaim in the late 1960s and early 1970s; his iconic work "Needle Tower" was created in 1968 and installed at the Hirshhorn Museum and Sculpture Garden. He was a student at Black Mountain College in 1948 and 1949, where he studied under Buckminster Fuller and Josef Albers. His work sits at the intersection of art, engineering, and science, placing him within the same mid-century milieu as the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, which promoted similar collaborations. In addition to his structural sculptures, Snelson was a renowned panoramic photographer and a researcher into atomic structures, for which he held several U.S. patents.

results.possibleIdentity: Otto Tambellini appears to be a historical reference to the collaborative artistic efforts of Otto Piene (1928–2014) and Aldo Tambellini (1930–2020), rather than a single individual. The name is frequently found in media art archives and digital databases as a shorthand for their joint work during the late 1960s and early 1970s. This duo is most famous for 'Black Gate Cologne' (1968), which is widely cited as the first television program created by artists. This project was an intermedia event involving light objects, experimental film, and electronic video mixing, produced at the 'Electronic Studio' of WDR television in Germany. Both artists were deeply involved in the intersection of art and technology and are associated with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which facilitated collaborations between artists and engineers. Individually, Otto Piene was a German artist and co-founder of the Group Zero, known for his light ballets and 'Sky Art,' while Aldo Tambellini was a pioneer in video art and experimental filmmaking who founded the Black Gate Theater in New York.

results.possibleIdentity: Ron Dubren (born c. 1943) is an American artist, inventor, and toy designer who was active in the New York experimental art scene during the late 1960s and 1970s. While pursuing a PhD in Clinical Psychology at New York University (which he completed in 1975), Dubren became involved with the city's avant-garde and video art movements. His work, such as the video piece titled "The Magic Box," was featured in the 11th and 12th Annual Avant Garde Festival of New York (1974–1975). These festivals were known for fostering collaborations between artists and technicians, often involving the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.). Dubren is listed in historical records from the period alongside notable figures such as Nam June Paik and Charlotte Moorman. In 1978, he transitioned from psychology to full-time creative work, initially as a puzzle writer for newspapers and later as a toy inventor. In 1980, he founded Ron Dubren & Associates and went on to achieve significant commercial success as the co-inventor of the "Tickle Me Elmo" toy, which utilized an electronic chip he originally developed for a different prototype. Throughout his career, he has continued to identify as an artist, later collaborating with contemporary artists like Alexa Meade on innovative toy designs and interactive projects.

results.possibleIdentity: Walter Wright is a pioneering video and computer artist who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s and is closely associated with the early intersections of art and technology. His work was significantly influenced by the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) organization, and he once noted that his involvement with E.A.T. allowed him to meet and collaborate with budding electronic music composers and engineers. Beginning his career in computer graphics around 1965, Wright developed early 3D software at the University of Waterloo in 1966. In the early 1970s, he worked as a computer animator for Computer Image Corporation, where he operated the Scanimate analog computer system to create video animations, including a collaboration on Ed Emshwiller's 'Scapemates' (1972). He served as one of the first artists-in-residence at the Experimental Television Center (ETC) starting in 1972 and was a key figure at The Kitchen in New York during its formative years (1971–1973). His artistic practice often involved the use of custom-built video synthesizers, such as his own 'Vidium' system, which he played like a musical instrument to generate real-time abstract imagery.

William Wright (often known as Bill Wright) was an artist and influential curator active in the 1960s and 1970s, primarily known for his work in Australia and his leadership in international contemporary art. While he is best remembered as the director of the 1982 Biennale of Sydney, he maintained a continuous artistic practice for over 60 years, producing monochromatic and textured mixed-media paintings. During the late 1960s and early 1970s, he lived in Europe, studying in Italy and other centers. In his curatorial role, he was a strong advocate for cross-disciplinary and experimental art forms, including video art and sound art, aligning with the interdisciplinary goals of organizations like Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.).

results.possibleIdentity: W. Sagher is likely a reference to Yoram Sagher, a mathematician and educator who collaborated with artists through the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) in the late 1960s and early 1970s. Sagher was a professor of mathematics at the University of Illinois Chicago (UIC) and the Weizmann Institute of Science. His most notable contribution to the art world was his collaboration with artist Robert Breer on the development of the 'Floats'—slow-moving, motorized, dome-shaped robotic sculptures. Sagher provided the mathematical analysis for the movement of these sculptures, which were a central feature of the Pepsi Pavilion at Expo '70 in Osaka, Japan, a major project organized by E.A.T. Sagher's name appears in E.A.T. directories and technical service lists from 1968 onwards, often as 'Yoram Sagher' or 'Sagher, Y.' The initial 'W.' in some records may result from misreading historical faculty directories or alphabetical lists where he appeared adjacent to individuals with that initial (such as Neil W. results.possibleIdentity: Dolores H. "Dilly" Schapiro (1930–2022) was a New York-based environmental designer, architect, and arts patron active during the late 1960s and 1970s. She attended the Columbia University School of Architecture and earned a bachelor's degree in environmental design from the Parsons School of Design in 1973. During the 1970s, she was on the staff of the West Side Highway Project, an initiative to upgrade the high-speed road along Manhattan's Hudson River shoreline, and worked for several New York architectural firms. Schapiro was a prominent arts enthusiast and a longtime patron of major New York cultural institutions, including the Museum of Modern Art (MoMA), the Whitney Museum of American Art, the Metropolitan Museum of Art, and the New York Philharmonic. Her association with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) is likely linked to her professional work and studies in environmental design and architecture, fields that were central to E.A.T.'s collaborative projects during that period.
